title: Building a simple papermaking vat
subtitle: Setting up a home-scale vat from household equipment
excerpt: A papermaking vat does not need to be a specialist item. A clean plastic storage box, a second-hand blender, and a few pieces of felt or J-cloth are enough to pull your first sheets. This tutorial covers vat sizing, water depth, and how to prepare cotton linter for a first batch.
sourceNotes: Home-scale papermaking setup guidance synthesised from general hand-papermaking practice. Cotton linter as a beginner pulp is established practice in the Western hand-papermaking revival tradition.

glossaryTerms.definition: Papermaking fibre that has been partially processed — cooked, beaten, or pre-softened — but not yet diluted to vat consistency. Cotton linter sheets are a form of half-stuff.
The proportion of pulp to water in the vat. A typical home-studio vat runs at about 0.5–1% pulp by weight: one blender load of pulp in 20 litres of water.

content.text: The papermaking vat is a shallow tub of water holding a dilute suspension of fibre. The mould and deckle is dipped into this suspension, pulled up through it, and lifted out carrying a thin layer of fibre that drains and mats into a sheet. The vat needs to be only three things: wider than the mould, low-sided enough to reach into comfortably, and clean.

attrs.title: Safety: blender use near water
attrs.body: Always unplug the blender before loading or rinsing. Never operate a mains-powered blender with wet hands. Keep the power lead away from the water surface.

items.name: Papermaking vat (slug: papermaking-vat)
items.description: A clean plastic storage box 40 × 30 cm or larger. Low sides (200, 250 mm) make it easier to reach in. A dedicated vat is better than a shared food container.

items.name: Paper mould and deckle (slug: paper-mould-deckle-a4)
items.description: The frame that forms the sheet. The mould (mesh-covered inner frame) does the work; the deckle (open outer frame) holds the pulp on the mould during the pull.

items.name: Cotton linter sheets (slug: cotton-linter)
items.description: Pre-processed cotton fibre sold as compressed dry sheets. Tear into pieces the size of a playing card, soak for 15 minutes, then blend.

items.name: Papermaking blender (slug: papermaking-blender)
items.description: A dedicated blender kept for studio use only. A standard kitchen blender works for cotton linter. Do not use the same blender for food after papermaking.

items.name: Couching cloth (slug: couching-cloth)
items.description: The cloth the freshly pulled sheet is transferred onto. J-cloths, wool felt, or tight-weave linen all work.

items.name: Papermaking sponge (slug: papermaking-sponge)
items.description: For pressing water out of the couched sheet from above.

items.name: Press boards (slug: papermaking-press-boards)
items.description: A pair of flat boards for pressing the sheet stack under weight.

content.text: Fill the vat with water to a depth of 200, 250 mm. This depth is enough to submerge the mould fully during the pull. Colder water drains more slowly than warm; room-temperature water gives the most predictable results. Hard tap water is fine for cotton linter. If you are pulling linen-rag pulp or recycled paper pulp, filtered water produces a cleaner sheet.
Cotton linter sheet is a form of

: partially processed fibre ready for blending. Tear half a sheet into small pieces and soak in warm water for 15 minutes. The linter will absorb water and soften. Load the blender to one-third full with water, add a handful of soaked linter, and blend on high for 30 to 45 seconds. The mixture should turn a smooth, milky white. Pour into the vat and add water to the working depth. Stir thoroughly: the pulp settles within a minute or two and needs stirring before every pull.

Dip the mould into the vat and pull it up through the pulp. If the sheet is full of holes, the vat is too dilute: add another blender load of pulp. If the sheet is thick and uneven, the vat is too concentrated: add water. One blender load of pulp in a 20-litre vat is a reasonable starting point. The first two or three sheets are diagnostic: use them to adjust consistency before pulling the sheets you want to keep.

Lay a couching cloth flat on the press board. Hold the draining mould at about 30 degrees and press the near edge onto the cloth. Roll the mould face-down onto the cloth in a smooth arc, pressing evenly. Lift the mould away cleanly: the sheet should release and lie flat on the cloth. Press from above with the sponge to drive out water, then cover with the second press board. Stack additional couched sheets (each with its cloth between) and press under heavy books or a block press for at least 30 minutes.
